---
title: "3.1.4 Instruction set"
subtitle: "Hardware P83-P84"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .two-col { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 1.6rem; align-items: start; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## Instruction set

### Instructions as decoded operations

In a computer system, <span class="term">instructions</span> (指令) are a set of operations which are decoded in sequence.

Each operation will instruct the <span class="term">ALU</span> and <span class="term">CU</span>, which are part of the CPU.

This links software execution directly to CPU hardware.

## Operation structure

### Opcode and operand

An operation is made up of an <span class="term">opcode</span> (操作码) and an <span class="term">operand</span> (操作数).

The opcode informs the CPU what operation needs to be done.

The operand is the data which needs to be acted on, or it can refer to a register in the memory.

## Opcode and operand

### Keep the two roles separate

::: {.two-col}
<div>
<span class="term">Opcode</span>

The part that tells the CPU the operation to carry out, such as add, jump or load.
</div>

<div>
<span class="term">Operand</span>

The data to be acted on, or a reference to a register or memory location.
</div>
:::

A strong answer should not describe both parts as “the instruction”.

## Instruction set

### Limited number of opcodes

Since the computer needs to understand the operation to be carried out, there is actually a limited number of opcodes that can be used.

This is known as the <span class="term">instruction set</span> (指令集).

All software running on a computer will contain a set of instructions, which need to be converted into <span class="term">binary</span> (二进制).

## Instruction set and FDE

### CPU processes instructions in sequence

The <span class="term">Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle</span> is the sequence of steps used by the CPU to process each instruction in sequence.

This means instruction sets should be understood as low-level operations that the CPU can decode and execute, not as high-level classroom pseudocode.

## X86 example

### Same instruction set, different electronic designs

One example of an instruction set is the <span class="term">X86</span>, a common CPU standard used in many modern computers.

Although different computer manufacturers will adopt their own internal electronic design, if the computer is based on the X86 CPU then all designs will share almost identical instruction sets.

## X86 example

### Intel Pentium and AMD Athlon

For example, <span class="term">Intel Pentium</span> and <span class="term">AMD Athlon</span> CPUs use almost identical X86 instruction sets even though they are based on very different electronic designs.

This is why the instruction set is a CPU standard issue, not simply a brand-name issue.

## Do not confuse instruction sets with program code

### Textbook caution

Do not confuse instruction sets with <span class="term">programming code</span> (程序代码).

Instruction sets are the low-level language instructions that instruct the CPU how to carry out an operation.

Program code needs <span class="term">interpreters</span> (解释器) or <span class="term">compilers</span> (编译器) to convert the code into the instruction set understood by the computer.

## Example instruction set operations

### ADD, JMP and LDA

Some examples of instruction set operations include:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">ADD</span>: add operation
- <span class="term">JMP</span>: jump operation
- <span class="term">LDA</span>: load accumulator operation
:::

These are low-level CPU instructions, not high-level programming statements.

## Classroom Check

### Define the three key terms

Students should be able to explain:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">opcode</span>: the operation to be done
- <span class="term">operand</span>: the data or register/memory reference used by the operation
- <span class="term">instruction set</span>: the limited set of operations the CPU can understand
:::

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
